buckets.title: Bucket 2: Mo's Algorithm (Standard, Rollback, on Trees) (Rating: 2100–2500)
buckets.goal: Master offline range queries. You will sort queries geographically into $\sqrt{N}$ blocks using custom comparators. When adding elements is easy but removing them is mathematically impossible (like maintaining a maximum), you will implement **Rollback Mo's**. Finally, you will flatten trees using Euler Tours to apply Mo's Algorithm directly to tree paths.

buckets.title: Bucket 3: Mo's with Updates (3D Mo's) & Block Decomposition on Trees (Rating: 2200–2800)
buckets.goal: The absolute limit of array partitioning. You will introduce a 3rd dimension (Time) to Mo's Algorithm to process point updates in $O(N^{5/3})$ time. You will also directly decompose the edges of a Tree into $\sqrt{N}$ discrete sub-trees, maintaining structures in each block to answer complex dynamic path queries.

subtopics.title: 26.1 — Foundations: Points and Vectors
bullets: **Representation**: point P = (x, y); vector v = (dx, dy) = P2 - P1
**Basic operations**:
Vector addition: (a1+b1, a2+b2)
Scalar multiplication: k×(a, b) = (ka, kb)
Vector negation: -(a, b) = (-a, -b)
**Dot product**: a · b = a.x×b.x + a.y×b.y = |a||b|cos(θ)
= 0: perpendicular; > 0: acute angle; < 0: obtuse angle
Projection of a onto b: (a · b) / |b|
Check if angle > 90°: just check sign of dot product
**Cross product (2D scalar)**: a × b = a.x×b.y - a.y×b.x = |a||b|sin(θ)
> 0: b is counterclockwise (left) of a
< 0: b is clockwise (right) of a
= 0: collinear (parallel)
|a × b| = area of parallelogram formed by a and b
**Length/magnitude**: |v| = √(v.x² + v.y²)
**Unit vector**: v / |v|
**Angle**: atan2(v.y, v.x) — returns angle in (-π, π]
**Numeric types**:
Integer coordinates (long long): use cross/dot products without float for exact orientation
Floating point (double/long double): necessary for lengths, angles; use epsilon comparisons
`eps = 1e-9` for comparisons: if |a - b| < eps → treat as equal
**Polar angle sort**: sort points by angle from reference; use cross product for comparison (no atan2 needed)

subtopics.title: 26.2 — Geometry Primitives
bullets: **Distance**:
Point to point: sqrt((x2-x1)² + (y2-y1)²)
Squared distance (integer-safe): (x2-x1)² + (y2-y1)²
Point to infinite line through P1 and P2: |cross(P2-P1, P-P1)| / |P2-P1|
Point to segment [P1, P2]: clamp parameter t = dot(P-P1, P2-P1) / |P2-P1|² to [0,1], then distance to P1 + t×(P2-P1)
**Collinearity**: cross(P2-P1, P3-P1) == 0
**Orientation of three points** (most used primitive):
cross(B-A, C-A) > 0: CCW; < 0: CW; = 0: collinear
Use long long to avoid overflow for integer inputs
**Perpendicular line**: direction perpendicular to (dx, dy) is (-dy, dx)
**Line-line intersection** (infinite lines):
Parameterize as P1 + t×(P2-P1) = P3 + s×(P4-P3); solve for t, s
t = cross(P3-P1, P4-P3) / cross(P2-P1, P4-P3)
Parallel if denominator = 0
**Segment-segment intersection**:
Check if orientation of endpoints straddles each other
orientation(A, B, C) × orientation(A, B, D) ≤ 0 AND orientation(C, D, A) × orientation(C, D, B) ≤ 0
Handle collinear case (overlap check) separately with bounding box check
**Point on segment**: collinear + bounding box (min/max x and y) check
**Reflection of point over line**: use dot product projection
**Rotating point by angle θ**: (x cosθ - y sinθ, x sinθ + y cosθ)

subtopics.title: 26.3 — Convex Hull
bullets: **Convex hull**: smallest convex polygon containing all given points
**Graham Scan** — O(n log n):
Find bottom-most point (break ties by leftmost) → pivot
Sort remaining points by polar angle from pivot (cross product comparator)
Sweep: maintain stack; pop while the last three points make a right turn or collinear
Handle collinear points carefully (include or exclude based on problem)
**Monotone Chain (Andrew's Algorithm)** — O(n log n) — preferred in CP:
Sort points lexicographically by (x, y)
Build lower hull: iterate left to right, pop if right turn
Build upper hull: iterate right to left, pop if right turn
Concatenate (remove duplicate endpoints)
Cleaner, easier to handle degenerate cases, numerically stable
Returns points in CCW order
**Jarvis March (Gift Wrapping)** — O(nh), h = hull size:
Start from leftmost point, repeatedly find point making smallest CCW angle
Good when h is small (h ≪ n), e.g., few hull points
**Convex hull of convex polygons** (Minkowski sum): O(n + m)
**Properties and operations on convex hull**:
Area via shoelace formula: A = ½|Σ(x[i]×y[i+1] - x[i+1]×y[i])|
Perimeter: sum of edge lengths
Diameter (farthest pair): rotating calipers O(n)
Checking if point is inside convex hull: O(log n) binary search on angular sectors
Support function: maximum dot product with given direction → binary search O(log n)
Tangent lines from external point: binary search for tangent vertex O(log n)

subtopics.title: 26.4 — Polygon Algorithms
bullets: **Shoelace formula** (signed area):
Signed area = ½ × Σ(x[i] × y[i+1] - x[i+1] × y[i])
Positive → CCW orientation; Negative → CW
Double to avoid fractions when using integers
**Perimeter**: sum of Euclidean distances between consecutive vertices
**Point in arbitrary polygon** (ray casting):
Cast ray from point in any direction (e.g., +x direction)
Count edge crossings; odd count → inside; even → outside
Handle edge/vertex cases carefully (e.g., ray coincides with edge)
O(n) time
**Point in convex polygon**: O(log n)
Binary search on angular sectors from a fixed interior vertex
Check if point is in the correct triangle sector, then if CCW from both bounding edges
**Convex polygon intersection**: O(n + m) — Sutherland-Hodgman clipping algorithm
Clip one polygon against each half-plane defined by the other's edges
**Area of union of polygons**: inclusion-exclusion + polygon intersection (complex)
**Pick's theorem**: Area = I + B/2 - 1
I = interior lattice points; B = boundary lattice points; Area = polygon area
B = sum of gcd(|dx|, |dy|) for each edge (dx, dy)
Useful to find I from Area and B, or vice versa

subtopics.title: 26.5 — Convex Hull Trick (CHT) Revisited (Geometry Perspective)
bullets: Maintaining lower envelope of lines: y = a[i]*x + b[i]
Each line corresponds to a "supporting" direction on the convex hull
Dynamic convex hull with insertions: Li Chao tree
This is also where CHT from DP section connects to geometry

subtopics.title: 26.6 — Sweep Line Algorithms
bullets: **Core concept**: sweep a vertical line from left to right, process events
**Event types**: segment start, segment end, intersection point
**Active set**: balanced BST (e.g., `std::set`) of active segments ordered by y-coordinate at current x
Must handle dynamic ordering (y-order changes as x changes)
Use segment comparator based on current x
**Segment intersection detection (Shamos-Hoey)** — O(n log n):
Insert left endpoints → add segment to active set
Remove right endpoints → delete from active set
Check neighbors of inserted/deleted segment for intersection
If any intersection found → return true; else false
**Area of union of rectangles** — O(n log n):
Events: left edge (add interval) and right edge (remove interval)
At each event, query total covered length of y-axis using segment tree with lazy propagation
Multiply covered length by horizontal gap to get area
**Closest pair of points** — O(n log n):
Sweep left to right; maintain active set of points within distance d of sweep line
For each new point, check O(1) candidates in the (2d × d) strip
Update d when a closer pair found; remove points more than d behind sweep line
**Voronoi diagram / Delaunay triangulation**: Fortune's sweep algorithm O(n log n) (concept; rarely implemented from scratch in CP)

subtopics.title: 26.7 — Rotating Calipers
bullets: **Concept**: simulate two parallel supporting lines rotating around the convex hull simultaneously
Maintain two antipodal points on the convex hull, rotating calipers synchronized
**Key insight**: as one caliper advances past one vertex, the other cannot go backward → O(n) total
**Diameter of convex polygon** — O(n) after hull construction:
Find farthest pair of hull vertices
Start with two hull vertices; advance the one with smaller "advance" (cross product comparison)
Track maximum distance throughout
**Width of convex polygon** (minimum distance between parallel supporting lines):
Similar rotating calipers, track minimum
**Minimum enclosing rectangle** of convex hull:
For each edge of hull, find bounding rectangle aligned with that edge via rotating calipers
O(n) with 4 rotating calipers (one per rectangle side)
**Minimum perimeter triangle**: rotating calipers on 3 hull vertices, O(n²)
**Maximum distance between two convex polygons**: rotating calipers on both polygons O(n + m)
**Minimum distance between two convex polygons**: rotating calipers on both O(n + m)

subtopics.title: 26.8 — Half-Plane Intersection
bullets: **Half-plane**: directed line L divides plane; left side is "inside"
Represented as line direction vector d and point on line p
A point X is inside if cross(d, X - p) ≥ 0
**Half-plane intersection (HPI)**: find convex region that is inside all given half-planes
**Applications**:
Find a point satisfying all linear constraints (2D LP)
Kernel of a polygon (set of points from which entire polygon is visible)
Feasibility of linear program
**Algorithm — Sort + Deque** — O(n log n):
Add new half-plane to back
While intersection point of last two back half-planes is outside the new half-plane, pop back
While intersection point of first two front half-planes is outside the new half-plane, pop front
Add to back
**Kernel of polygon**: apply HPI with half-planes from each polygon edge (inside = left of CCW edge)
Non-empty kernel → polygon is star-shaped (exists point seeing all of polygon)
**Binary search on HPI**: if answer is monotone function of some parameter → binary search + HPI feasibility check

subtopics.title: 26.9 — Closest Pair of Points — O(n log n)
bullets: **Naive**: check all C(n,2) pairs: O(n²)
**Divide and Conquer**:
Sort by x; split at median
Recurse on left and right halves → get d_L and d_R; d = min(d_L, d_R)
Merge step: check points within distance d of dividing line (the "strip")
Sort strip points by y, check each against ≤ 7 subsequent points (packing argument)
O(n log n) total (log n recursion levels, O(n) work per level)
**Sweep-line approach** (simpler to code):
Sort by x; maintain active set ordered by y
For each new point, remove points with x < current_x - d from active set
Check O(1) points in [current_y - d, current_y + d] range in active set
O(n log n)
**Randomized approach**: shuffle → O(n) expected with incremental algorithm

subtopics.title: 26.10 — Circle Algorithms
bullets: **Circle representation**: center (cx, cy) and radius r
**Distance checks**:
Circle-circle: d = dist(c1, c2); disjoint if d > r1+r2; one inside other if d < |r1-r2|; intersect otherwise
Point in circle: dist(P, C) ≤ r
Point on circle: dist(P, C) == r (with epsilon)
**Circle-line intersection**: project center onto line, check distance vs radius
**Circle-segment intersection**: point-to-segment distance ≤ radius (and intersection point on segment)
**Common tangent lines** between two circles:
External tangents: 2 if circles don't overlap
Internal tangents: 2 if circles don't intersect at all
Formulas using distance and radii
**Tangent lines from external point** to circle:
Length of tangent: sqrt(dist(P, C)² - r²)
Angle: arcsin(r / dist(P, C))
**Circle-circle intersection points**: standard formula using law of cosines
**Area of circle segment** and sector
**Minimum enclosing circle (Welzl's algorithm)**: O(n) expected
Randomly shuffle points, incrementally add; if new point outside current circle, recompute with it on boundary
Three cases: 1 point on boundary, 2 points on boundary, 3 points on boundary
Expected O(n) due to random order

subtopics.title: 26.11 — 3D Geometry Basics
bullets: **3D Cross product**: a × b = (a.y×b.z - a.z×b.y, a.z×b.x - a.x×b.z, a.x×b.y - a.y×b.x) — returns vector
**3D Dot product**: a · b = a.x×b.x + a.y×b.y + a.z×b.z — returns scalar
**Volume of tetrahedron**: (1/6) × |a · (b × c)| where a, b, c are edge vectors from one vertex
**Normal of plane**: cross product of two vectors in the plane
**Plane equation**: ax + by + cz = d; (a, b, c) is normal vector
**Point-plane distance**: |ax + by + cz - d| / sqrt(a² + b² + c²)
**Ray-plane intersection**: parametric ray P = O + t×D; solve for t
**Convex hull in 3D**: harder; gift wrapping O(nf) or randomized O(n log n), f = faces

subtopics.title: 26.12 — Numerical Precision in Geometry
bullets: **Integer geometry**: whenever possible, use long long arithmetic for orientation, collinearity, area
Cross products: only multiplication and subtraction → no float errors
Watch for overflow: if coordinates up to 10^6, cross product can reach 10^12 → need long long
If coordinates up to 10^9, cross product can reach 10^18 → need __int128 or careful handling
**Float geometry**: use double or long double; define eps = 1e-9
Comparison: `sign(x) = (x > eps) - (x < -eps)` returns -1, 0, or 1
Never compare floats for exact equality
**Common pitfall**: degenerate cases — collinear points, zero-length segments, coincident points
**Exact arithmetic libraries** (rarely used in CP): Java's BigDecimal, Python's Fraction
**When to use integers vs floats**:
Orientation tests, area computations (factor of 2 trick): integers
Angles, distances (sqrt needed), circle intersections: floats

buckets.title: Bucket 1: Convex Hull & Polygon Primitives (Rating: 2000–2400)
buckets.goal: Establish your geometric baseline. These problems test your ability to implement Andrew's Monotone Chain and basic polygon queries (area, point-in-polygon) entirely using `cross_product` and integer math to avoid precision loss.

buckets.title: Bucket 2: Lines, Calipers & Half-Plane Intersection (Rating: 2200–2700)
buckets.goal: Dynamic geometric optimizations. You will implement Half-Plane Intersection (HPI) via the $O(N \log N)$ Sort-and-Incremental algorithm to find feasible regions, and use Rotating Calipers to find diameters of convex hulls.

buckets.title: Bucket 3: Advanced Sweep Line, Voronoi & Delaunay (Rating: 2500–3000)
buckets.goal: The absolute summit of geometry. You will maintain Binary Search Trees of active segments during radial and vertical line sweeps. You will also map the dual graphs of Delaunay Triangulations (Voronoi Diagrams) to partition a plane by nearest neighbors.

subtopics.title: 27.1 — CDQ Divide and Conquer (Offline)
bullets: **CDQ D&C** (Chen Dan Qi's divide & conquer): solve problems with multi-dimensional partial order offline
**Core idea**: divide the operation sequence in half; use left half's updates to answer right half's queries
**3D partial order problem** (canonical CDQ application):
Given n points (a, b, c); for each point, count how many other points have all three coordinates strictly less
Naive: O(n²) or O(n² log n); CDQ: O(n log² n)
Step 1: sort by first dimension (a)
Step 2: CDQ D&C on second dimension, merge sort on third dimension, BIT for counting
Total: O(n log n) for each level × O(log n) levels = O(n log² n)
**CDQ for 2D**: sort by one dimension, CDQ on time (insertion order) → O(n log n) replacing O(n log² n) BIT
**Inverting CDQ**: instead of left updates → right queries, sometimes use right queries ← left updates (difference)
**CDQ vs. persistent data structures**: CDQ is offline but simpler; persistent allows online but uses more memory
**Applications**:
Count inversions (2D partial order): CDQ gives clean O(n log n) solution
3D partial order for DP transition counting
Dynamic range queries that would require 2D segment tree online
Certain graph problems where edges arrive over time

subtopics.title: 27.2 — Dynamic DP (DDP) with HLD
bullets: **Problem**: tree DP, but edges/weights change dynamically; re-running DP each time is O(n) per update
**Goal**: maintain tree DP under updates in O(log² n) per update and query
**Matrix formulation**:
Write DP recurrence as matrix/semiring product
Classic example: max weighted independent set
dp[v][0] = max independent set not including v = Σ max(dp[c][0], dp[c][1]) for c in children(v)
dp[v][1] = weight[v] + Σ dp[c][0] for c in children(v)
This can be written as: state(v) = A(v) × state(child) for some matrix A(v) (using (max, +) semiring)
**Using HLD**:
HLD decomposes tree into chains
Each chain = sequence of matrices → maintain product with segment tree over chain
Each segment tree node stores product of matrices in its range
Update: change one matrix (one node's weight) → O(log n) segment tree updates per chain × O(log n) chains = O(log² n)
Query: root value = product of matrices along root's chain × contribution of heavy children = O(log² n)
**Matrices used** (for max independent set):
2×2 matrices where entry (i, j) = contribution if parent is in state i and child chain starts in state j
(max, +) semiring multiplication
**Complications**:
Light edges: handled separately (they don't form a chain)
The "top" of each chain interacts with its parent via heavy edge
Root value requires aggregating across all chains
**Applications**:
Dynamic max independent set on tree
Dynamic path DP with weight changes
Any tree DP with linear recurrence structure that can be expressed as matrix product

subtopics.title: 27.3 — Explicit Treap (Detailed Implementation)
bullets: **Structure**: each node has key k, random priority p, left/right children, subtree size, lazy tags
**Heap + BST**: max-heap on priority (randomly assigned at creation) → balanced with high probability
**Core operations**:
**Split(t, k) → (L, R)**: split treap t into L (keys ≤ k) and R (keys > k)
**Merge(L, R) → t**: merge two treaps (all keys in L < all in R)
**Insert(k)**: split at k, create new node, merge back: O(log n) expected
**Delete(k)**: split at k-1 and k, merge the two non-k parts: O(log n) expected
**Kth element**: descend using subtree sizes: O(log n) expected
**Count less than k**: split + size + merge back: O(log n) expected
**Implicit Treap** (position-based, no explicit keys):
Key = position in sequence (implicit; not stored)
Split by size: "split first k elements from the rest"
Maintains sequence of elements with O(log n) insert, delete, split, merge
**Lazy propagation on implicit treap**: range reverse, range add, range assign
**Range reverse** in O(log n): split + set rev flag on middle + merge
**Full power**: any sequence operation in O(log n)
**Treap vs Segment Tree**: treap supports insert/delete at arbitrary positions; segment tree doesn't

subtopics.title: 27.4 — Splay Tree
bullets: **Self-adjusting BST**: every accessed node is moved to root via "splay" operation
**Splay operation**: sequence of rotations — zig, zig-zig, zig-zag:
**Zig**: single rotation (parent is root)
**Zig-zig**: both node and parent are same-sided children → rotate parent first, then node
**Zig-zag**: node and parent are opposite-sided children → rotate node twice
**Amortized O(log n)** per splay + access; worst case single operation O(n) but amortized O(n log n) for n operations
**Access sequence property**: recently accessed nodes stay near root (temporal locality)
**Operations**:
Find(k): binary search + splay k to root
Insert(k): BST insert + splay to root
Delete(k): find k, splay to root, merge left and right subtrees
Split at k: splay k to root; left subtree = keys < k, right subtree = keys > k
**Splay tree vs Treap**:
Both O(log n) amortized; Splay has better cache locality; Treap is simpler and usually faster in practice
Splay is required for Link-Cut Trees (LCT)
**Link-Cut Trees (LCT)**: advanced dynamic tree structure built on splay trees
Supports link(u,v), cut(u,v), find_root(v), path_aggregate(u,v) — all O(log n) amortized
Preferred tree data structure for online dynamic connectivity + path queries
Implementation: preferred path decomposition via "access" operation; splay preferred paths
Applications: online MST, dynamic forests, reachability under edge insertions/deletions

subtopics.title: 27.5 — Wavelet Tree
bullets: **Structure**: persistent segment tree built on value range, not index range
**Build**: sort by value range midpoint at each level; O(n log V) time and space
**Operations** — all O(log V):
Kth smallest in index range [l, r]: descend left/right based on count of elements in left half
Count elements ≤ v in [l, r]: sum of counts going left when value ≤ midpoint
Count elements in value range [v1, v2] and index range [l, r]: two count queries
kth element in [l, r] with value in [v1, v2]: combination of above
**Advantage over merge sort tree**: same O(log V) per query; simpler than fractional cascading
**Advantage over persistent segment tree**: built on value range so specific query types are more natural
**Applications**:
Kth smallest in range (classic)
Count distinct values in range
Inverse table queries
LCS length in O(n log n) (via counting inversions using wavelet)

subtopics.title: 27.6 — Parallel Binary Search
bullets: **Problem**: answer Q queries each requiring binary search on the same structure
**Naive**: Q × O(log n × check_cost) — too slow if check is expensive
**Parallel binary search**: answer all Q queries simultaneously in O(log n) rounds
**Algorithm**:
Each query q maintains [lo_q, hi_q] — current binary search range
In each round: for each query, compute mid_q = (lo_q + hi_q) / 2
Group queries by their current midpoint
Process all queries in one pass through the structure (e.g., add edges one by one)
At each event (adding edge), answer all queries with mid at this point
Based on result, update lo or hi for each query
After O(log n) rounds, all queries converged
**Complexity**: O(log n × (structure_build_cost + Q))
**Applications**:
Kth ancestor queries offline (binary search on depth)
Find first time a condition is satisfied (e.g., first time two nodes connected in DSU)
Online → offline query conversion
Offline minimum spanning tree queries

buckets.title: Bucket 1: CDQ Divide & Conquer & Dimensionality Reduction (Rating: 2500–2800)
buckets.goal: Master "Divide and Conquer over Time." You will take offline queries that normally require 2D/3D Segment Trees (like point-add/rectangle-sum or dynamic connectivity) and split them in half. You compute how the *past* updates affect the *future* queries, completely eliminating the need for nested data structures.

buckets.title: Bucket 2: Dynamic DP (DDP) & HLD Matrix Acceleration (Rating: 2700–3100)
buckets.goal: Accelerate tree-based Dynamic Programming. You will flatten trees using Heavy-Light Decomposition (HLD) and represent your DP transitions as $2 \times 2$ or $3 \times 3$ matrices. You then build a Segment Tree over the HLD paths to multiply these matrices, allowing you to update DP states across the tree in $O(\log^2 N)$.

problems.name: Can you answer these queries VII

buckets.title: Bucket 3: Treap, Splay Trees & Link-Cut Trees (Rating: 2800–3300)
buckets.goal: The absolute summit of dynamic data structures. You will use Treaps to cut and paste array segments, and Link-Cut Trees (which use internal Splay Trees) to dynamically add and remove edges from a forest while simultaneously answering path queries in $O(\log N)$. This is where the legendary `QTREE` series resides.

Dynamic Tree Vertex Set Path Composite
Dynamic Tree Vertex Add Subtree Sum

subtopics.title: 28.1 — Linear Basis (XOR Basis)
bullets: **XOR basis**: set of vectors over GF(2) such that any element of the span can be uniquely decomposed
**Construction**: greedy reduction — for each element, try to reduce using existing basis vectors
**Standard form (row echelon)**: each basis vector has a unique leading bit; O(log max_val) size basis
**Maximum XOR of any subset**: greedily pick basis elements to maximize
**Check if value v is achievable (in span)**: try to reduce v using basis; if v = 0 → yes
**Kth element in sorted order of all XOR combinations** (with reduced row echelon form)
**Merge of two bases**: insert all elements of smaller basis into larger: O(log² max_val)
**Applications**:
Maximum XOR subarray (prefix XOR + linear basis query)
Count subsets with XOR = target
Maximize XOR sum with exactly k elements
Network flow with XOR constraints

subtopics.title: 28.2 — Gaussian Elimination
bullets: **Over reals (GE)**: solve Ax = b in O(n³); find rank, basis of null space
Partial pivoting: swap rows to put largest element in pivot position (numerical stability)
Back substitution to find solution
Detect: no solution (inconsistent) or infinitely many solutions (underdetermined)
**Gaussian Elimination over GF(2)** (XOR systems):
Matrix entries are bits; row operations use XOR instead of subtraction
Pivot: find row with leading 1 in column, XOR into all other rows
O(n² × m / 64) with bitset optimization
**Applications**:
Lights-out puzzle and variants (toggle switches)
Find subset of vectors with XOR = target
Minimum number of XOR operations
Linear recurrence solving over GF(2)
**Polynomial Gaussian Elimination**: solve systems of polynomial equations (symbolic)

subtopics.title: 28.3 — Matrix Exponentiation (Deep Dive)
bullets: **Semiring generalization**: works over any semiring, not just (+, ×)
(min, +) semiring: shortest paths in O(V³ log k)
(max, +) semiring: longest paths, DP recurrences
(or, and) semiring: reachability in k steps
(max, min) semiring: bottleneck paths
**Linear recurrences**: if f(n) = c1×f(n-1) + c2×f(n-2) + ... + ck×f(n-k), find f(n) in O(k³ log n)
Companion matrix: k×k matrix encoding the recurrence
General: nth term = [companion matrix]^n × [initial vector]
**Counting paths of exactly k edges**: A^k gives counts in O(V³ log k)
**Fibonacci variants**:
Every n, every other: matrix exponentiation with modified recurrence
Tribonacci, Tetranacci: extend companion matrix
Fibonacci modulo period (Pisano period): precompute period, then use modulo
**Transfer matrix method** for tiling:
State = profile of current column
Count ways to tile n×m grid: O(2^m × n) or O(2^m × m² × log n) with matrix expo
**Important optimization**: if n ≤ 50 or so, matrix size up to 50×50 feasible; k up to 10^18 → log k ≤ 60 multiplications → 50³ × 60 ≈ 7.5×10^8 — might be tight, optimize matrix multiply

subtopics.title: 28.4 — Formal Power Series (FPS) Operations
bullets: **Setting**: work with truncated power series mod x^n; use NTT for O(n log n) multiplication
**NTT-friendly prime**: 998244353 = 119 × 2^23 + 1 (primitive root g = 3)
Supports NTT up to 2^23 elements
**Polynomial inverse** f^{-1} mod x^n — O(n log n) via Newton's iteration:
Base: (1/f) mod x^1 = modular_inverse(f[0])
Doubling: if g ≡ f^{-1} mod x^k, then g' = 2g - f×g² ≡ f^{-1} mod x^{2k}
Repeat until mod x^n
**Polynomial division** f/g mod x^n = f × g^{-1} mod x^n
**Polynomial log** ln(f) mod x^n — requires f[0] = 1:
ln'(f) = f'/f; integrate
ln(f) = integral(f' × f^{-1}) mod x^n — O(n log n)
**Polynomial exp** exp(f) mod x^n — requires f[0] = 0:
Newton's method: if G ≡ exp(f) mod x^k, then G' = G × (1 + f - ln(G)) mod x^{2k}
O(n log n) via iterated NTT
**Polynomial sqrt** √f mod x^n — requires f[0] = 1 (or f[0] is a perfect square):
Newton's method: G' = (G + f × G^{-1}) / 2 mod x^{2k}
O(n log n)
**Polynomial power** f^k mod x^n:
If f[0] = 1: f^k = exp(k × ln(f)) — O(n log n)
If f[0] ≠ 0: extract leading term, reduce to f[0] = 1 case
If f[0] = 0: find first non-zero coefficient, handle separately
**Applications**:
Compute nth coefficient of generating functions
EGF operations: counting labeled structures
Partition function: sum of products via log/exp
Implicit function theorem: solving equations involving power series
Multipoint evaluation: evaluate polynomial at n points in O(n log² n) using product tree

subtopics.title: 28.5 — Fast Walsh-Hadamard Transform (FWT/XOR Convolution)
bullets: **Problem**: given arrays A and B, compute C where C[k] = Σ A[i] × B[j] for i XOR j = k
**Naive**: O(n²); FWT: O(n log n)
**Transform**: FWT(A)[k] = Σ A[i] × (-1)^{popcount(i AND k)}
Butterfly transform, similar to FFT but with ± instead of roots of unity
**AND convolution**: C[k] = Σ A[i] × B[j] for i AND j = k — use AND-FWT (OR-FWT on complements)
**OR convolution**: C[k] = Σ A[i] × B[j] for i OR j = k — use Zeta/Möbius transforms (SOS DP)
**Implementation**: iterative butterfly, O(n log n)
**Applications**:
XOR convolution for subset XOR problems
AND convolution for problems involving shared bits
Combining SOS DP with convolutions

subtopics.title: 28.6 — Matroid Theory (Applied)
bullets: **Matroid definition**: (E, I) where E = ground set, I = independent sets:
∅ ∈ I (empty set is independent)
Hereditary: A ∈ I, B ⊆ A → B ∈ I
Exchange: A, B ∈ I, |B| > |A| → ∃ e ∈ B\A: A ∪ {e} ∈ I
**Rank function**: r(S) = max size of independent subset of S
**Common matroids**:
**Graphic matroid**: G = (V, E); I = acyclic edge subsets (forests); r(S) = |V| - components(S)
**Linear matroid**: vectors in F^n; I = linearly independent subsets; r(S) = dim(span(S))
**Partition matroid**: partition E into groups; I = at most k_i from group i
**Uniform matroid**: U(r, n); I = all subsets of size ≤ r
**Transversal matroid**: bipartite G; I = sets matchable from left side
**Greedy algorithm on matroids**: sort by weight, greedily add if still independent → maximum weight independent set
This is Kruskal's algorithm for graphic matroid!
**Matroid intersection** — find maximum common independent set of two matroids:
Unweighted: augmenting paths in exchange graph; O(r² × oracle_calls) where r = rank
Weighted: slower; binary search on weight + augmenting paths
Oracle: independence test for each matroid
**Applications**:
Rainbow spanning forest: edges colored, each color class is a partition matroid; intersection with graphic matroid
Arborescences (directed spanning trees): intersection of two graphic matroids
Colorful independent sets
Common base of two matroids (most powerful)

subtopics.title: 28.7 — Lifting the Exponent Lemma (LTE)
bullets: **v_p(n)**: largest power of prime p dividing n (p-adic valuation)
**LTE Lemma** (for odd prime p, p | a - b, p ∤ a, p ∤ b):
v_p(a^n - b^n) = v_p(a - b) + v_p(n)
v_p(a^n + b^n) = v_p(a + b) + v_p(n) when n is odd
**LTE for p = 2** (2 | a - b, i.e., a and b same parity):
v_2(a^n + b^n) = v_2(a + b) if 2 ∤ n
**Applications**:
Prove divisibility: show n | a^k - b^k
Find v_p of large expressions without computing them
Problems asking for largest power of prime dividing some expression
**Common patterns**: a^n - 1 = (a-1)(a^{n-1} + ... + 1); LTE to find v_p of each factor

subtopics.title: 28.8 — Polynomial Identity Testing & Randomization
bullets: **Schwartz-Zippel Lemma**: if P(x1,...,xk) is a non-zero polynomial of degree d, then Pr[P(r1,...,rk) = 0] ≤ d/|S| when ri chosen uniformly from a set S
Choose S large (e.g., prime ≥ 10^9), evaluate at random point
If value is 0 → likely P ≡ 0; if non-zero → P ≢ 0 with certainty
**Applications**:
Verify if two polynomial expressions are identical: O(1) evaluations after O(n) preprocessing
Bipartite matching via polynomial (Schwartz-Zippel for perfect matching detection)
Planar graph testing heuristics
**Freivalds' Algorithm**: verify A × B = C in O(n²) using random vector v:
Check if A × (Bv) = Cv; if yes → probably true; if no → definitely false
Repeat k times: probability of false positive ≤ 1/2^k
**Random hashing for geometry**: project high-dimensional objects to 1D via random dot product
**Isolation Lemma**: randomly weight elements to make minimum weight solution unique (used in parallel matching)

buckets.title: Bucket 1: XOR Linear Basis & Gaussian Elimination over GF(2) (Rating: 2400–2900)
buckets.goal: Vector spaces applied to binary. You must stop viewing XOR as a bitwise operator and start viewing it as addition in a vector space modulo 2. You will compute the linear basis of arrays and graphs to answer maximum-subset-XOR queries and cycle-basis shortest paths in strictly $O(\log(\text{MAX\_A}))$ time.

buckets.title: Bucket 2: Matrix Exponentiation Deep Dive & Semirings (Rating: 2500–2900)
buckets.goal: Overloading the matrix operator. You will transition from standard dot products to multiplying matrices under arbitrary semirings (like Min-Plus or Boolean OR-AND). You will use this to tile massive grids, compute graph paths of exact length $10^{18}$, and transition Automata states in $O(S^3 \log K)$.

buckets.title: Bucket 3: Formal Power Series (FPS) (Rating: 2900–3300)
buckets.goal: The absolute peak of combinatorics. You will represent infinite sequence recurrences as algebraic polynomials. Using Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) and Number Theoretic Transform (NTT), you will compute the Inverse, Logarithm, Exponential, and Square Root of polynomials modulo $998244353$ to count structures in $O(N \log N)$.

buckets.title: Bucket 4: FWT / XOR Convolution (Rating: 2800–3200)
buckets.goal: Accelerating logic gates. You will use the Fast Walsh-Hadamard Transform (FWT) to multiply polynomials where the "exponent addition" is replaced by bitwise XOR, AND, or OR. This allows you to count combinations of subsets that yield a specific bitwise mask in strictly $O(N 2^N)$.

buckets.title: Bucket 5: Matroid Theory & Matroid Intersection (Rating: 3000–3500)
buckets.goal: The holy grail of combinatorial optimization. You will use Matroid Intersection algorithms to find the maximum independent set satisfying *two* distinct structural constraints simultaneously (e.g., a set of edges that is both a forest AND has maximum degrees bounded by $K$).

buckets.title: Bucket 6: Lifting The Exponent (LTE) & Schwartz-Zippel Lemma (Rating: 2500–3000)
buckets.goal: Advanced number theory and randomized identity testing. You will use the LTE Lemma to extract prime valuations from exponential differences without calculating the massive numbers themselves. You will deploy the Schwartz-Zippel Lemma to verify polynomial/matrix equality in $O(N^2)$ using random assignments instead of deterministic $O(N^3)$.
